crime: As a child she had been a blade-throwing prodigy. The skill had left her.

A younger prisoner — a blade prodigy the whole prison gathers to watch — admired her as the old master, and had arranged a weekend demonstration: the two of them throwing side by side, for everyone to see.
The girl practiced alone in the courtyard at dusk, throwing at a mark near the fountain.
The other prisoners knew her habit: she walked the second-floor railing over the courtyard for the thrill, balanced out over the drop, rosin on her palms for grip.
At dusk she stepped onto the railing above the girl; her hands came back. She threw a single practice-knife — palmed earlier from the girl's own set — down into her, and stepped in off the railing.
The girl was found dead by the courtyard fountain, a thrown blade in her, struck steeply from above.
The wound's steep angle placed the throw on the second-floor railing — from on top of it, out over the drop. Fresh scuffs and a smear of hand-rosin marked the top rail.
Her account: her hands are gone; the throw is beyond her. The railing over the drop is the edge she alone walks, and at that edge, her hands return.
fields.epitaph: She was no one until she was about to die.
fields.epithet: She Who Shone Only at the Edge
execution: A narrow ledge jutted from the execution stand over a shaft two stories deep, the floor bare stone. She was set out on it.
Behind her, the ledge began to draw back, strip by strip.
Her hands found no grip. No old skill came to them.
The last strip drew back, and nothing was under her.

magic.name: Edge-Bright
magic.text: When she steps into true mortal danger by her own act, the skill she lost comes back in full while she holds that edge.
